title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a choir leader called George talking about a community singing project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a choir leader called George talking about a community singing project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A choir leader called George talks about a community singing project.
extracts.audio_script: Good evening. I’m George, the director of Riverbank Community Choir. The choir began seven years ago when a few neighbours wanted to sing together without needing auditions or formal music training. They first met in the back room of a café, but the group soon became too large. Our weekly rehearsals are now held in an old [9]boat house[/9] beside the river, which has been renovated as a community venue. Its high wooden roof produces a warm sound, although we have to close the doors in winter because it can become chilly.||We welcome adults and students aged fifteen or over. Nobody is required to read music. At the first rehearsal, new singers are invited to try different parts of the choir and speak to an experienced member. Our director used to begin with breathing exercises only, but we now start by helping everyone find their [10]vocal range[/10], so people join the section in which they feel most comfortable. Members do not need special clothing at rehearsals. Please bring a bottle of water and a [11]pencil[/11], because we sometimes make small changes to words or mark where a song becomes quieter. Printed music is loaned to you during the term.||The choir performs several kinds of music. We sing traditional songs, music from films and a few modern arrangements. Members voted on the focus for this term. Although several people supported songs from musicals, the greatest number chose [12]folk music[/12], particularly pieces with local connections. We have also invited a musician to join some rehearsals. Rather than using a piano for every piece, our new accompanist will play the [13]violin[/13], which suits this season’s songs especially well. Rehearsals take place on Wednesday evenings, and recordings are available online for members who want to practise between meetings.||Performing in front of an audience can be worrying at first. New members are therefore not expected to sing a solo or stand in the front row. Our first performance each year is deliberately informal and takes place at the town [14]food festival[/14], where audiences come and go throughout the afternoon. Singers can relax and gain experience. People who want to build confidence may also attend a short [15]stage skills workshop[/15] led by a theatre teacher. It covers posture, looking at the conductor and managing nerves, rather than advanced singing technique.||As a community group, we try to support others. Members who attend at least eighty per cent of rehearsals receive a free [16]concert recording[/16] at the end of term, created by one of our sound volunteers. In December, our major public performance will take place in the [17]town cathedral[/17], an impressive building with enough room for a large audience. Ticket income will not be used for new costumes or travel. This year it will support a local [18]mental health charity[/18], which runs creative activities for teenagers. Rehearsals start next month, and there are information sheets beside the door for anyone considering joining.

dialogue.speaker: george
